Sketch walkthrough

Aggressive B-NHL — Burkitt & DLBCL Therapy

🎯 Look at the lit symbol — say out loud what it is and what it represents (and the trap). Then reveal.

What you see
What it encodes
The board trap
Use ← → arrow keys · click a dot to jump · ▶ plays & narrates the scene
⚠️ Board traps 12
  1. Burkitt: MYC, Ki-67 ~95% — Standard R-CHOP undertreats Burkitt — its rapid growth needs intensive regimens (R-hyperCVAD or DA-EPOCH-R for select cases).
  2. Shortest doubling time — The faster the doubling time, the higher the tumor lysis risk — Burkitt mandates aggressive rasburicase + hydration prophylaxis.
  3. Endemic vs sporadic — A jaw mass in an African child = endemic Burkitt (EBV+); an abdominal mass in a Western adult = sporadic — same biology, different presentation.
  4. DA-EPOCH-R (BCL2-negative) — DA-EPOCH-R is favored in double-hit/high-grade lymphoma over R-CHOP, which underperforms in MYC-rearranged disease.
  5. R-CHOP (DLBCL backbone) — R-CHOP cures the majority of DLBCL — it is curative-intent, unlike palliative management of indolent FL.
  6. Pola-R-CHP — In Pola-R-CHP, vincristine (the 'O') is removed — adding it back would compound neurotoxicity.
  7. CAR-T (relapsed DLBCL) — For early relapse (<12 mo) or primary-refractory DLBCL, CAR-T beats autologous transplant — don't default to ASCT salvage.
  8. Relapse timing switch (12 mo) — Time-from-treatment is the fork — late chemosensitive relapse still favors ASCT; early relapse routes to CAR-T.
  9. Response rate 80-90% — High response rate isn't the same as cure — a positive end-of-treatment PET (Deauville 4-5) signals residual disease needing salvage.
  10. Salvage chemo — If salvage fails to achieve chemosensitivity, proceed to CAR-T rather than transplanting refractory disease.
  11. R-EPOCH reinforced (high-grade) — Adding CNS prophylaxis matters in high-grade/MYC disease and Burkitt — omitting it risks CNS relapse.
  12. Novel agents (POLA, BiTE, ADC) — Bispecifics, like CAR-T, can cause cytokine release syndrome and ICANS — recognize fever/hypotension post-infusion as CRS, not sepsis alone.
📖 Read-through — all 12 symbols
1. Burkitt: MYC, Ki-67 ~95%
Burkitt lymphoma — MYC t(8;14), Ki-67 near 100%, fastest doubling time of any tumor. Starry-sky histology. Curable but demands intensive, dose-dense therapy.
⚠️ Standard R-CHOP undertreats Burkitt — its rapid growth needs intensive regimens (R-hyperCVAD or DA-EPOCH-R for select cases).
2. Shortest doubling time
Burkitt has a doubling time often <24-48h — the highest proliferative fraction of human tumors. This drives extreme tumor lysis risk and the need to start therapy urgently.
⚠️ The faster the doubling time, the higher the tumor lysis risk — Burkitt mandates aggressive rasburicase + hydration prophylaxis.
3. Endemic vs sporadic
Endemic Burkitt (Africa, EBV+, jaw/facial mass), sporadic (Western, abdominal/ileocecal mass), immunodeficiency-associated (HIV). All share MYC translocation and aggressive course.
⚠️ A jaw mass in an African child = endemic Burkitt (EBV+); an abdominal mass in a Western adult = sporadic — same biology, different presentation.
4. DA-EPOCH-R (BCL2-negative)
Dose-adjusted EPOCH-R is an option for Burkitt and for double-hit/high-grade B-cell lymphoma. Infusional dosing exploits proliferation kinetics; CNS prophylaxis is required.
⚠️ DA-EPOCH-R is favored in double-hit/high-grade lymphoma over R-CHOP, which underperforms in MYC-rearranged disease.
5. R-CHOP (DLBCL backbone)
R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) is curative first-line for most DLBCL, ~6 cycles. ~60-70% cure overall depending on IPI.
⚠️ R-CHOP cures the majority of DLBCL — it is curative-intent, unlike palliative management of indolent FL.
6. Pola-R-CHP
Polatuzumab (anti-CD79b ADC) substituted for vincristine (R-CHP) improved progression-free survival in higher-risk DLBCL (POLARIX). Vincristine is dropped to avoid overlapping neuropathy.
⚠️ In Pola-R-CHP, vincristine (the 'O') is removed — adding it back would compound neurotoxicity.
7. CAR-T (relapsed DLBCL)
CD19 CAR-T (axi-cel/liso-cel) is standard for relapsed/refractory DLBCL. ZUMA-7/TRANSFORM showed superiority to salvage chemo + transplant in early relapse (<12 months).
⚠️ For early relapse (<12 mo) or primary-refractory DLBCL, CAR-T beats autologous transplant — don't default to ASCT salvage.
8. Relapse timing switch (12 mo)
Relapse timing dictates salvage: late relapse (>12 months) → salvage chemo + autologous stem cell transplant if chemosensitive; early/primary-refractory → CAR-T.
⚠️ Time-from-treatment is the fork — late chemosensitive relapse still favors ASCT; early relapse routes to CAR-T.
9. Response rate 80-90%
Aggressive curable B-NHL achieves high overall response rates with first-line immunochemotherapy. Interim/end PET (Deauville) assesses metabolic response.
⚠️ High response rate isn't the same as cure — a positive end-of-treatment PET (Deauville 4-5) signals residual disease needing salvage.
10. Salvage chemo
Platinum-based salvage (R-ICE, R-DHAP) is used to re-induce chemosensitivity before autologous transplant in eligible relapsed DLBCL. Chemorefractory disease portends CAR-T.
⚠️ If salvage fails to achieve chemosensitivity, proceed to CAR-T rather than transplanting refractory disease.
11. R-EPOCH reinforced (high-grade)
Dose-intensified infusional regimens are reserved for double-hit/high-grade and CNS-risk disease, with intrathecal CNS prophylaxis. Reflects higher proliferative burden.
⚠️ Adding CNS prophylaxis matters in high-grade/MYC disease and Burkitt — omitting it risks CNS relapse.
12. Novel agents (POLA, BiTE, ADC)
Bispecific T-cell engagers (epcoritamab, glofitamab targeting CD20×CD3), antibody-drug conjugates (polatuzumab, loncastuximab), and tafasitamab+lenalidomide expand relapsed/refractory options.
⚠️ Bispecifics, like CAR-T, can cause cytokine release syndrome and ICANS — recognize fever/hypotension post-infusion as CRS, not sepsis alone.
